title: The Storm-Archive Beyond Signal

features.name: the-broadcast-made-water
features.desc-on-touch: Rain that holds the weight of all the radio signals that have passed through this place. Each drop carries a fragment of a pirate broadcast, a voice that learned to travel on water instead of air. When it falls, it speaks—but only if you're listening through soil.

features.name: the-envelope-archives
features.desc-on-touch: Paper that has swollen with rain but not dissolved—envelopes bearing no addresses, containing fragments from The Leather Distributed. Each envelope holds something that was never meant to be opened but has been: a voice, a material, a question addressed to a person who is also you.

features.name: the-canopy-speaker
features.desc-on-touch: Leaves arranged as if they were the diaphragm of a speaker, broadcasting downward into the soil. The rain falls through them and becomes voice, voice falls upward through them and becomes weather. The forest is learning to listen to itself.

features.name: the-signal-memory-ground
features.desc-on-touch: Soil that has absorbed so many years of transmitted signal that it's become conductive in emotional ways. When you place your hand here, you feel the root-memory of broadcasts: pirate radios, payphone conversations, the hum of old equipment nobody uses anymore.

features.name: the-leather-binding-storm
features.desc-on-touch: Leather that has been left out through many storms and absorbed their memory—bound now into a volume that you can't quite hold, that feels wet and alive, that whispers when the wind passes. The participatory archive is now participating in weather.

features.name: the-silence-where-radio-ends
features.desc-on-touch: A clarity in the forest where no broadcast reaches, where the radio static has finally resolved into meaning and that meaning is the sound of rain on eucalyptus leaves. The signal ended not in static but in ecology; the archive became weather; the message became the messenger.

inscription: what weather remembers when radio falls silent is older than memory and newer than thought

file.name: FieldRecording_Rain_Through_Canopy_Filtered_Voices_Undated.wav.txt
file.note: spectral analysis of a 40-minute recording of rain that seems to carry speech; the voices are in no known language; the language is weather

reverie.title: REVERIE — the forest where the radio learned to stop talking
narr: you sit with her under the oldest eucalyptus as the
rain comes, and the radio broadcast in the distance
fades into static, fades into actual weather. she says:
this is what the archive wanted all along—not to be
heard, but to become the hearing itself. the signal
turning back to rain. the message becoming its own silence.

exits.label: back down toward where weather still pretends to be signal
deeper into the canopy where the forest is still learning to broadcast
toward the coast where rain becomes river becomes ocean
back into settled country where the signal still reaches
